expanded_description: Reciprocal trigonometric functions (secant, cosecant, cotangent) complete the set of six trigonometric functions. While sine, cosine, and tangent are the primary functions, their reciprocals (csc = 1/sin, sec = 1/cos, cot = 1/tan) appear frequently in calculus, physics, and engineering. Understanding reciprocal functions is essential for calculus (derivatives and integrals of all six functions), physics (certain formulas use sec, csc, cot), and advanced trigonometry (identities and equations). The reciprocal functions have unique graphs with vertical asymptotes where the primary functions equal zero. Their domains exclude values where the primary functions are zero (division by zero). In Pre-calculus, mastering reciprocal functions completes understanding of all six trig functions, prepares students for trigonometric identities (many involve reciprocals), and prepares for calculus (all six functions have derivatives and integrals). The reciprocal relationships are simple but powerful—knowing sin gives you csc immediately, knowing cos gives you sec, knowing tan gives you cot. This section teaches reciprocal functions—definitions, graphs, properties, evaluation, and connections to primary functions. Understanding all six trig functions builds complete trigonometric literacy.

core_concept.principle:
Reciprocal Trig Functions:

Cosecant: csc θ = 1/sin θ = hypotenuse/opposite
Secant: sec θ = 1/cos θ = hypotenuse/adjacent
Cotangent: cot θ = 1/tan θ = adjacent/opposite

Relationships to Primary Functions:
csc θ = 1/sin θ (reciprocal of sine)
sec θ = 1/cos θ (reciprocal of cosine)
cot θ = 1/tan θ (reciprocal of tangent)

Key Properties:
- Undefined when primary function = 0
- Vertical asymptotes where sin = 0 (csc), cos = 0 (sec), tan = 0 (cot)
- Range: |csc θ| ≥ 1, |sec θ| ≥ 1 (never between -1 and 1)
- cot θ can be any real number
- Period: csc and sec have period 2π, cot has period π

Right Triangle Definitions:
csc θ = hypotenuse/opposite
sec θ = hypotenuse/adjacent
cot θ = adjacent/opposite

Reciprocal Identities:
sin θ = 1/csc θ
cos θ = 1/sec θ
tan θ = 1/cot θ
core_concept.explanation: Think of reciprocal trig functions as the 'flip' of the primary functions—csc is 1/sin, sec is 1/cos, cot is 1/tan. This is simple but powerful—when sin is small, csc is large; when sin = 0, csc is undefined (vertical asymptote). The graphs reflect this—csc and sec have U-shaped branches that go to infinity near asymptotes, cot has repeating curves. The range restriction (|csc| ≥ 1, |sec| ≥ 1) comes from the fact that sin and cos are between -1 and 1, so their reciprocals must be ≥ 1 or ≤ -1. In right triangles, the reciprocal functions are just different side ratios—instead of opp/hyp (sin), you have hyp/opp (csc). This is useful in certain applications where the reciprocal ratio is more natural. In calculus, all six functions have derivatives and integrals—you need to know all six. Understanding reciprocals completes your trig toolkit—you can work with any trig function. This section teaches reciprocal functions—definitions, graphs, properties, and how they relate to primary functions.

image.alt: Overview of reciprocal trigonometric functions showing relationships and graphs
image.caption: Figure 7.7.1: Visual summary of reciprocal trigonometric functions: csc = 1/sin, sec = 1/cos, cot = 1/tan, with graphs showing asymptotes and key features.

sections.introduction: The three reciprocal trigonometric functions are cosecant (csc), secant (sec), and cotangent (cot). Each is the reciprocal of a primary trig function—csc is reciprocal of sin, sec is reciprocal of cos, cot is reciprocal of tan. Understanding these relationships is fundamental to working with all six trig functions.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.7.2: Introducing the three reciprocal trigonometric functions and their relationships to primary functions.

key_concept.definition:
Three Reciprocal Functions:

Cosecant (csc):
csc θ = 1/sin θ
Undefined when sin θ = 0

Secant (sec):
sec θ = 1/cos θ
Undefined when cos θ = 0

Cotangent (cot):
cot θ = 1/tan θ = cos θ/sin θ
Undefined when tan θ = 0 (or sin θ = 0)

Right Triangle Definitions:
csc θ = hypotenuse/opposite
sec θ = hypotenuse/adjacent
cot θ = adjacent/opposite

Reciprocal Identities:
sin θ · csc θ = 1
cos θ · sec θ = 1
tan θ · cot θ = 1

When Undefined:
csc θ undefined: θ = 0°, 180°, 360°, ... (sin = 0)
sec θ undefined: θ = 90°, 270°, 450°, ... (cos = 0)
cot θ undefined: θ = 0°, 180°, 360°, ... (tan = 0)
key_concept.context: Reciprocal functions complete the set of six trig functions. In calculus, all six have derivatives and integrals—you need to know all six. In physics, certain formulas use sec, csc, cot (especially in optics and wave mechanics). In engineering, reciprocal functions appear in certain applications. The reciprocal relationships are simple but powerful—knowing one function gives you its reciprocal immediately. The undefined points (where primary function = 0) create vertical asymptotes in the graphs. Understanding where functions are undefined is crucial for graphing and for calculus (limits, continuity). This section teaches reciprocal definitions and relationships—the foundation for working with all six trig functions.

image.caption: Figure 7.7.3: Diagram showing reciprocal relationships between all six trigonometric functions.

worked_examples.title: Finding Reciprocal Function Values

image.caption: Figure 7.7.4: Finding cosecant, secant, and cotangent values from sine, cosine, and tangent.

worked_examples.problem: If sin θ = 1/2, cos θ = √3/2, and tan θ = √3/3, find csc θ, sec θ, and cot θ.

step_by_step.calculation:
csc = hyp/opp, sec = hyp/adj, cot = adj/opp
Should give same values
csc θ = 2, sec θ = 2√3/3, cot θ = √3

worked_examples.final_answer: csc θ = 2, sec θ = 2√3/3, cot θ = √3
worked_examples.concept_applied: Reciprocal functions: csc = 1/sin, sec = 1/cos, cot = 1/tan; rationalize denominators when needed.

worked_examples.title: Finding Where Reciprocal Functions are Undefined

image.alt: Example finding where reciprocal functions are undefined
image.caption: Figure 7.7.5: Finding where cosecant, secant, and cotangent are undefined.

worked_examples.problem:
Find where each reciprocal function is undefined on [0°, 360°]:
a) csc θ
b) sec θ
c) cot θ

step_by_step.step: Find Where csc θ is Undefined
step_by_step.calculation:
sin θ = 0 at θ = 0°, 180°, 360°
csc undefined at 0°, 180°, 360°

step_by_step.step: Find Where sec θ is Undefined
step_by_step.calculation:
cos θ = 0 at θ = 90°, 270°
sec undefined at 90°, 270°

step_by_step.step: Find Where cot θ is Undefined
step_by_step.calculation:
tan θ = 0 at θ = 0°, 180°, 360°
cot undefined at 0°, 180°, 360°

worked_examples.concept_applied: Reciprocal functions undefined where primary function = 0; find zeros of sin, cos, tan.

worked_examples.title: Using Right Triangle Definitions

image.caption: Figure 7.7.6: Using right triangle definitions to find reciprocal trig values.

worked_examples.problem: In a right triangle with opposite = 3, adjacent = 4, hypotenuse = 5, find csc θ, sec θ, and cot θ.

step_by_step.explanation: Check using reciprocal of primary functions.
step_by_step.calculation:
sin = 3/5, so csc = 5/3 ✓
cos = 4/5, so sec = 5/4 ✓
tan = 3/4, so cot = 4/3 ✓

worked_examples.concept_applied: Right triangle definitions: csc=hyp/opp, sec=hyp/adj, cot=adj/opp; verify using reciprocals of primary functions.

image.caption: Figure 7.7.7: Summary of reciprocal trigonometric function definitions and relationships.

sections.introduction: The graphs of reciprocal trig functions (csc, sec, cot) have distinctive features—vertical asymptotes where the primary functions equal zero, and U-shaped branches that approach the asymptotes. Understanding these graphs is essential for visualizing reciprocal function behavior and for calculus (limits, continuity, asymptotes).

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.7.8: Introducing graphs of cosecant, secant, and cotangent functions.

key_concept.definition:
Cosecant Graph (y = csc x):
- Reciprocal of sine
- Vertical asymptotes where sin x = 0 (x = 0, π, 2π, ...)
- U-shaped branches above and below
- Range: (-∞, -1] ∪ [1, ∞)
- Period: 2π
- Undefined at x = nπ (n is integer)

Secant Graph (y = sec x):
- Reciprocal of cosine
- Vertical asymptotes where cos x = 0 (x = π/2, 3π/2, ...)
- U-shaped branches opening up and down
- Range: (-∞, -1] ∪ [1, ∞)
- Period: 2π
- Undefined at x = π/2 + nπ

Cotangent Graph (y = cot x):
- Reciprocal of tangent
- Vertical asymptotes where tan x = 0 (x = 0, π, 2π, ...)
- Decreasing curves in each period
- Range: (-∞, ∞) (all real numbers)
- Period: π
- Undefined at x = nπ

Key Features:
- Asymptotes where primary function = 0
- Never between -1 and 1 for csc and sec
- Reciprocal relationship visible in graph shapes
key_concept.context: Reciprocal function graphs are important for calculus—asymptotes, limits, continuity. The vertical asymptotes occur where the primary function equals zero (division by zero). The U-shaped branches of csc and sec come from the reciprocal relationship—when sin or cos is small, csc or sec is large. Cotangent's decreasing curves come from the reciprocal of tangent's increasing curves. Understanding these graphs builds visual understanding of reciprocal relationships. In applications, these graphs appear in wave mechanics, optics, and certain physics problems. This section teaches reciprocal graphs—asymptotes, shapes, periods, and key features.

image.caption: Figure 7.7.9: Diagram showing graphs of csc x, sec x, and cot x with asymptotes and key features labeled.

worked_examples.title: Sketching Cosecant Graph

image.caption: Figure 7.7.10: Sketching the graph of y = csc x.

worked_examples.problem: Sketch one period of y = csc x, showing asymptotes and key features.

step_by_step.calculation:
sin x = 0 at x = 0, π, 2π
Asymptotes at x = 0, π, 2π

Branch above x-axis from 0 to π (minimum at π/2)
Branch below x-axis from π to 2π (maximum at 3π/2)
W-shaped graph with asymptotes at multiples of π

worked_examples.final_answer: Graph of csc x: U-shaped branches with asymptotes at x = 0, π, 2π; minimum at (π/2, 1), maximum at (3π/2, -1)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Cosecant graph: reciprocal of sine, asymptotes where sin = 0, U-shaped branches, never between -1 and 1.

worked_examples.title: Sketching Secant Graph

image.caption: Figure 7.7.11: Sketching the graph of y = sec x.

worked_examples.problem: Sketch one period of y = sec x, showing asymptotes and key features.

step_by_step.calculation:
cos x = 0 at x = π/2, 3π/2
Asymptotes at x = π/2, 3π/2

Branch opening up from -π/2 to π/2 (minimum at 0)
Branch opening down from π/2 to 3π/2 (maximum at π)
U-shaped branches opening up and down with asymptotes at π/2 + nπ

worked_examples.final_answer: Graph of sec x: U-shaped branches opening up/down with asymptotes at x = π/2, 3π/2; minimum at (0, 1), maximum at (π, -1)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Secant graph: reciprocal of cosine, asymptotes where cos = 0, U-shaped branches, never between -1 and 1.

worked_examples.title: Sketching Cotangent Graph

image.caption: Figure 7.7.12: Sketching the graph of y = cot x.

worked_examples.problem: Sketch one period of y = cot x, showing asymptotes and key features.

step_by_step.calculation:
tan x = 0 at x = 0, π, 2π
Asymptotes at x = 0, π, 2π

Decreasing curve from asymptote at 0 to asymptote at π
Passes through (π/4, 1) and (3π/4, -1)
Decreasing curves with asymptotes at multiples of π, period π

worked_examples.final_answer: Graph of cot x: Decreasing curves with asymptotes at x = 0, π; passes through (π/4, 1) and (3π/4, -1); period π
worked_examples.concept_applied: Cotangent graph: reciprocal of tangent, asymptotes where tan = 0, decreasing curves, period π.

image.caption: Figure 7.7.13: Summary of reciprocal trigonometric function graphs.

sections.introduction: Evaluating reciprocal trig functions uses the reciprocal relationships—csc = 1/sin, sec = 1/cos, cot = 1/tan. For special angles (30°, 45°, 60°), you can find exact values using the special triangle values. For other angles, use a calculator. Understanding how to evaluate reciprocals is essential for all trig work.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.7.14: Introducing evaluation of reciprocal trigonometric functions.

key_concept.definition:
Evaluation Methods:

Method 1: Using Reciprocal Relationships
csc θ = 1/sin θ
sec θ = 1/cos θ
cot θ = 1/tan θ

Method 2: Using Right Triangle Ratios
csc θ = hypotenuse/opposite
sec θ = hypotenuse/adjacent
cot θ = adjacent/opposite

Special Angle Values:
30° (π/6):
csc = 2, sec = 2√3/3, cot = √3

45° (π/4):
csc = √2, sec = √2, cot = 1

60° (π/3):
csc = 2√3/3, sec = 2, cot = √3/3

Using Calculator:
- Use reciprocal keys (1/sin, 1/cos, 1/tan)
- Or calculate primary function, then take reciprocal
- Check calculator mode (DEG vs. RAD)

Undefined Values:
- csc undefined when sin = 0
- sec undefined when cos = 0
- cot undefined when tan = 0
key_concept.context: Evaluating reciprocal functions is straightforward—take reciprocal of primary function. For special angles, memorize the values (or derive from special triangles). For other angles, use calculator. The undefined points are important—where primary function = 0, reciprocal is undefined (vertical asymptote in graphs). In calculus, you'll need to evaluate all six functions, so knowing reciprocals is essential. In physics and engineering, certain formulas use sec, csc, cot directly. Understanding evaluation builds fluency with all six trig functions. This section teaches evaluation—using reciprocals, special angles, and calculator.

image.caption: Figure 7.7.15: Diagram showing methods for evaluating reciprocal trigonometric functions.

worked_examples.title: Evaluating Reciprocal Functions for Special Angles

image.alt: Example evaluating reciprocals for special angles
image.caption: Figure 7.7.16: Evaluating cosecant, secant, and cotangent for special angles.

worked_examples.problem:
Evaluate exactly (no calculator):
a) csc 30°
b) sec 45°
c) cot 60°
worked_examples.concept_applied: Special angles: use reciprocal of primary function values; rationalize denominators when needed.

worked_examples.title: Evaluating Reciprocal Functions Using Calculator

image.caption: Figure 7.7.17: Using calculator to evaluate reciprocal trigonometric functions.

worked_examples.problem:
Evaluate using calculator (round to 4 decimal places):
a) csc 25°
b) sec 50°
c) cot 75°

step_by_step.explanation: Ensure calculator is in degree mode.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Calculator evaluation: find primary function value, take reciprocal; check calculator mode (DEG vs. RAD).

worked_examples.title: Finding Undefined Values

image.caption: Figure 7.7.18: Finding where reciprocal trigonometric functions are undefined.

worked_examples.problem:
Find all values in [0°, 360°] where each function is undefined:
a) csc θ
b) sec θ
c) cot θ

step_by_step.step: Find Where csc θ is Undefined
step_by_step.calculation:
sin θ = 0 at θ = 0°, 180°, 360°
csc undefined at 0°, 180°, 360°

step_by_step.step: Find Where sec θ is Undefined
step_by_step.calculation:
cos θ = 0 at θ = 90°, 270°
sec undefined at 90°, 270°

step_by_step.step: Find Where cot θ is Undefined
step_by_step.calculation:
tan θ = 0 at θ = 0°, 180°, 360°
cot undefined at 0°, 180°, 360°

step_by_step.explanation: List all undefined points for each function.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Reciprocal functions undefined where primary function = 0; find zeros of sin, cos, tan in given interval.

image.caption: Figure 7.7.19: Summary of evaluating reciprocal trigonometric functions.

sections.introduction: Reciprocal trig functions satisfy important identities that relate them to the primary functions and to each other. These identities are essential for simplifying expressions, solving equations, and proving other identities. Understanding reciprocal identities completes understanding of all six trig functions.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.7.20: Introducing reciprocal trigonometric identities and relationships.

key_concept.definition:
Reciprocal Identities:
sin θ = 1/csc θ
cos θ = 1/sec θ
tan θ = 1/cot θ
csc θ = 1/sin θ
sec θ = 1/cos θ
cot θ = 1/tan θ

Quotient Identities (involving reciprocals):
tan θ = sin θ/cos θ
cot θ = cos θ/sin θ

Pythagorean Identities (involving reciprocals):
1 + cot²θ = csc²θ
tan²θ + 1 = sec²θ

Even/Odd Identities:
csc(-θ) = -csc θ (odd function)
sec(-θ) = sec θ (even function)
cot(-θ) = -cot θ (odd function)

Cofunction Identities:
csc(90° - θ) = sec θ
sec(90° - θ) = csc θ
cot(90° - θ) = tan θ
key_concept.context: Reciprocal identities are fundamental to trigonometry—they connect all six functions. In calculus, you'll use these identities for derivatives and integrals of all six functions. In solving trig equations, these identities help simplify and solve. In proving identities, these are often the first identities to use. Understanding all six functions and their relationships builds complete trigonometric literacy. This section teaches reciprocal identities—relationships between all six trig functions.

image.caption: Figure 7.7.21: Diagram showing all reciprocal trigonometric identities and relationships.

worked_examples.title: Using Reciprocal Identities to Simplify

image.caption: Figure 7.7.22: Using reciprocal identities to simplify trigonometric expressions.

step_by_step.calculation: sin θ · csc θ = sin θ · (1/sin θ)

worked_examples.concept_applied: Reciprocal identity: sin · csc = 1; undefined where sin = 0.

worked_examples.title: Using Pythagorean Identities with Reciprocals

image.alt: Example using Pythagorean identities with reciprocals
image.caption: Figure 7.7.23: Using Pythagorean identities involving reciprocal functions.

worked_examples.problem: If sec θ = 5/3 and θ is in QI, find tan θ.

step_by_step.explanation: θ is in QI, tan positive in QI.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Pythagorean identity with sec: tan²θ + 1 = sec²θ; determine sign from quadrant.

worked_examples.title: Proving Identity Using Reciprocals

image.caption: Figure 7.7.24: Proving trigonometric identity using reciprocal identities.

worked_examples.problem: Prove the identity: csc θ · cos θ = cot θ

step_by_step.explanation: Work with left side of equation.

step_by_step.calculation: Identity proved: csc θ · cos θ = cot θ

worked_examples.final_answer: Identity proved: csc θ · cos θ = cot θ
worked_examples.concept_applied: Proving identities: use reciprocal and quotient identities to transform one side to match the other.

image.caption: Figure 7.7.25: Summary of reciprocal trigonometric identities and relationships.

sections.section_title: 7.7.5 Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Working with reciprocal trig functions introduces specific pitfalls related to undefined values, reciprocal relationships, calculator use, and identity manipulation. Recognizing common mistakes helps students develop careful habits and produce accurate results. Understanding where errors typically occur enables self-correction and better problem-solving.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.7.26: Overview of common errors with reciprocal trigonometric functions.

key_concept.definition:
Common Errors:

1. Undefined Value Errors:
- Not recognizing where reciprocals are undefined
- Trying to evaluate csc where sin = 0
- Not identifying vertical asymptotes

2. Reciprocal Relationship Errors:
- Confusing csc with cos (similar names)
- Wrong reciprocal (csc ≠ 1/cos)
- Mixing up which is reciprocal of which

3. Calculator Errors:
- Using csc, sec, cot keys incorrectly
- Wrong calculator mode (DEG vs. RAD)
- Not taking reciprocal of primary function

4. Identity Errors:
- Wrong Pythagorean identity with reciprocals
- Forgetting which identity uses which reciprocal
- Sign errors in identities

5. Graph Errors:
- Not drawing asymptotes
- Drawing graph between -1 and 1 for csc/sec
- Wrong curve shape for cotangent

Error Prevention:
- Memorize which is reciprocal of which
- Check where primary function = 0 (undefined)
- Use calculator carefully (reciprocal of primary)
- Memorize Pythagorean identities with reciprocals
- Draw asymptotes on graphs
- Remember csc/sec never between -1 and 1
key_concept.context: Reciprocal function errors are common—confusing csc with cos (similar names), not recognizing undefined points, wrong calculator use. Identity errors come from not memorizing which Pythagorean identity uses which reciprocal. Graph errors come from not understanding the reciprocal relationship (csc and sec can't be between -1 and 1). Understanding these errors develops reciprocal function literacy—knowing relationships, undefined points, and graph features. In calculus, these errors lead to wrong derivatives and integrals. This section teaches error awareness—critical for accurate reciprocal function work.

image.caption: Figure 7.7.27: Diagram showing common errors with reciprocal trigonometric functions and how to avoid them.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Reciprocal Relationship Error

image.caption: Figure 7.7.28: Correcting errors in reciprocal relationships.

worked_examples.problem: A student wrote csc θ = 1/cos θ. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.calculation:
Student confused csc with sec
csc is reciprocal of sin, not cos

csc ↔ sin (both have 's')
sec ↔ cos (both have 'c' sound)
cot ↔ tan (both have 't')

worked_examples.concept_applied: Reciprocal relationships: csc=1/sin, sec=1/cos, cot=1/tan; use mnemonics to remember.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Undefined Value Error

image.caption: Figure 7.7.29: Correcting errors with undefined values.

worked_examples.problem: A student tried to evaluate csc 180° and got 1. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.calculation:
Cannot divide by zero
csc 180° is undefined

step_by_step.explanation: This corresponds to vertical asymptote on graph.
step_by_step.calculation: Graph has vertical asymptote at x = 180°

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: csc 180° = 1; Correct: csc 180° is undefined (sin 180° = 0)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Reciprocal functions undefined where primary function = 0; check for division by zero.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Calculator Mode Error

image.caption: Figure 7.9.30: Correcting errors from wrong calculator mode.

worked_examples.problem: A student calculated sec 60° and got -2. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.explanation: Calculator is likely in radians mode.

step_by_step.calculation: Set calculator to DEG mode for degree problems

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: calculator in RAD mode; Correct: sec 60° = 2 (set to DEG mode)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Always check calculator mode (DEG vs. RAD) matches problem (degrees vs. radians).

image.caption: Figure 7.7.31: Summary of common errors with reciprocal trigonometric functions.

sections.section_title: 7.7.6 Comprehensive Practice with Reciprocal Trig Functions
sections.introduction: Apply all skills related to reciprocal trig functions—definitions, graphs, evaluation, identities, and error avoidance—to demonstrate mastery. These problems integrate multiple concepts and prepare students for advanced trigonometry and calculus.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Practice Set

image.caption: Figure 7.7.32: Practice problems covering reciprocal trigonometric functions.

worked_examples.problem:
Complete the following problems:

1) csc = 1/?
2) sec = 1/?
3) cot = 1/?
4) csc 30° = ?
5) sec 45° = ?
6) cot 60° = ?
7) Where is csc undefined?
8) Where is sec undefined?
9) Period of cot?
10) Range of sec?
11) csc θ · sin θ = ?
12) sec²θ - 1 = ?
13) 1 + cot²θ = ?
14) csc(-θ) = ? (even/odd)
15) sec(-θ) = ? (even/odd)
16) Graph has asymptotes where sin = ?
17) csc can never be between ? and ?
18) cot 45° = ?
19) sec 60° = ?
20) csc 90° = ?

step_by_step.step: 17) csc can never be between ? and ?

worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive practice demonstrating mastery of reciprocal trigonometric functions—definitions, values, identities, properties.

image.caption: Figure 7.7.33: Detailed solutions to all practice problems.

summary: Reciprocal trig functions: csc = 1/sin, sec = 1/cos, cot = 1/tan. Right triangle: csc=hyp/opp, sec=hyp/adj, cot=adj/opp. Undefined where primary function = 0 (csc where sin=0, sec where cos=0, cot where tan=0). Graphs: csc and sec have U-shaped branches with asymptotes, never between -1 and 1; cot has decreasing curves. Periods: csc and sec have period 2π, cot has period π. Identities: sin·csc=1, cos·sec=1, tan·cot=1; 1+cot²=csc², tan²+1=sec². Special angles: csc 30°=2, sec 45°=√2, cot 60°=√3/3. Even/odd: csc and cot are odd, sec is even. Common errors: confusing csc with cos, not recognizing undefined values, wrong calculator mode, wrong identities. Reciprocal functions complete the six trig functions—essential for calculus (all six have derivatives/integrals), physics, and engineering. Understanding reciprocals builds complete trigonometric literacy.

review_questions_image.alt: Comprehensive summary: reciprocal trig functions, definitions, graphs, identities, special values, common errors
review_questions_image.caption: Figure 7.7.34: Complete reference for reciprocal trigonometric functions showing definitions, graphs, identities, special angle values, and key properties.
